title: CO₂ emissions per capita vs. fossil fuel consumption per capita
subtitle: Fossil fuel consumption is measured as the average consumption of energy from coal, oil and gas per person. [Fossil fuel and industry emissions](#dod:fossilemissions) are included. [Land-use change emissions](#dod:land-use-change-emissions) are not included.
caption:
In 2024, the relationship between per capita fossil fuel energy consumption and per capita CO2 emissions shows a strong positive correlation across nations, with both axes plotted on logarithmic scales. The horizontal axis measures energy from coal, oil, and gas in kilowatt-hours per person, ranging from under 5,000 kWh to over 100,000 kWh, while the vertical axis measures emissions in tonnes per person, spanning from under 1 tonne to over 20 tonnes. Data points are color-coded by income group, with pink representing lower-middle-income countries, light green for upper-middle-income countries, and dark green for high-income countries, while circle size corresponds to population.

The distribution forms a diagonal band moving from the bottom-left to the top-right, indicating that higher energy consumption coincides with higher emissions. At the lower end of the scale, Pakistan and the Philippines sit in the bottom-left corner, with energy consumption below 5,000 kWh and emissions under 1 tonne per person. Moving up the scale, India and Indonesia appear as large circles in the lower-middle range, with India positioned around 10,000 kWh and 2 tonnes of emissions. China stands out as a massive circle in the middle of the chart, located near 30,000 kWh and roughly 8 tonnes of emissions.

High-income countries dominate the upper-right quadrant. The United States, South Korea, and the Netherlands cluster in a band between 50,000 and 70,000 kWh, with emissions ranging from 10 to 15 tonnes. Further to the right, Singapore and Saudi Arabia show energy consumption exceeding 100,000 kWh per person, with emissions between 10 and 20 tonnes. Qatar occupies the highest position on the chart, with emissions exceeding 20 tonnes per person and energy consumption well above 100,000 kWh. The visual pattern confirms that across all income groups, increased per capita fossil fuel usage aligns directly with increased per capita carbon output.
